name: Girth and Length-plus-Girth
description: Parcel girth (the distance around the package's two smaller sides) and length plus girth (longest side plus girth). Parcel carriers use length plus girth to set maximum-size limits and oversize surcharges.

outputs.name: Girth
outputs.description: 2 x (width + height) — the distance around the package perpendicular to its longest side.

outputs.name: Length plus girth
outputs.description: Longest side plus girth — the figure carriers compare against their size limits.

assumptions: The package is a rectangular box and L is its longest side.
Dimensions are outer dimensions as shipped, including packaging.
The formula is unit-agnostic (inches or centimetres), as long as all three dimensions use the same unit.
limitations: Carrier size limits vary by carrier, service, and date — e.g. UPS publishes a 165 in length-plus-girth maximum and 108 in maximum length (checked 2026-07-02); confirm current limits before booking.
Some carriers use girth alone rather than length plus girth — Australia Post's size guidelines cap girth by itself.
Carriers may round or re-measure dimensions, which can push a borderline package over a limit.

examples.note: 40 x 30 x 20 in package: girth = 2 x (30 + 20) = 100 in; length plus girth = 40 + 100 = 140 in.

sources: UPS 'Shipping Dimensions and Weight' support page (ups.com/us/en/support/shipping-support/shipping-dimensions-weight, checked 2026-07-02): girth = 2 x width + 2 x height; packages up to 165 in in length and girth combined, up to 108 in in length.

Australia Post size and weight guidelines (auspost.com.au/business/shipping/guidelines/size-weight-guidelines, checked 2026-07-02): Australia Post measures girth alone (not length plus girth) against its parcel size limits.
